Of Novatus and his Followers. The Novatians of Phrygia alter the Time of keeping Easter, following Jewish Usage.

ccel.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

title: Of Novatus and his Followers. The Novatians of Phrygia alter the Time of keeping Easter, following Jewish Usage.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: About this time the Novatians inhabiting Phrygia changed the day for celebrating the Feast of Easter.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: How this happened I shall state, after first explaining the reason of the strict discipline which is maintained in their church, even to the present day, in the provinces of Phrygia and Paphlagonia.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Novatus, a presbyter of the Roman Church, separated from it, because Cornelius the bishop received into communion believers who had sacrificed during the persecution which the Emperor Decius had raised against the Church.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Having seceded on this account, on being afterwards elevated to the episcopacy by such bishops as entertained similar sentiments, he wrote to all the churches that ‘they should not admit to the sacred mysteries those who had sacrificed; but exhorting them to repentance, leave the pardoning of their offense to God, who has the power to forgive all sin.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Receiving such letters, the parties in the various provinces, to whom they were addressed, acted according to their several dispositions and judgments.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: As he asked that they should not receive to the sacraments those who after baptism had committed any deadly sin this appeared to some a cruel and merciless course: but others received the rule as just and conducive to the maintenance of discipline, and the promotion of greater devotedness of life.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: In the midst of the agitation of this question, letters arrived from Cornelius the bishop, promising indulgence to delinquents after baptism.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Thus as these two persons wrote contrary to one another, and each confirmed his own procedure by the testimony of the Divine word, as it usually happens, every one identified himself with that view which favored his previous habits and inclinations.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Those who had pleasure in sin, encouraged by the license then granted them, took occasion from it to revel in every species of criminality.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Now the Phrygians appear to be more temperate than other nations, and are seldom guilty of swearing.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The Scythians, on the other hand, and the Thracians, are naturally of a very irritable disposition: while the inhabitants of the East are addicted to sensual pleasures.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But the Paphlagonians and Phrygians are prone to neither of these vices; nor are the sports of the circus and theatrical exhibitions in much estimation among them even to the present day.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: And for this reason, it seems to me, these people, as well as others of the same character, so readily assented to the letters then written by Novatus.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Fornication and adultery are regarded among them as the grossest enormities: and it is well known that there is no race of men on the face of the earth who more rigidly govern their passions in this respect than the Phrygians and Paphlagonians.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The same reason I think had force with those who dwelt in the West and followed Novatus.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Yet although for the sake of stricter discipline Novatus became a separatist, he made no change in the time of keeping Easter, but invariably observed the practice that obtained in the Western churches.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For they celebrate this feast after the equinox, according to the usage which had of old been delivered to them when first they embraced Christianity.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: He himself indeed afterwards suffered martyrdom in the reign of Valerian, during the persecution which was then raised against the Christians.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But those in Phrygia who are named after him Novatians, about this period changed the day of celebrating Easter, being averse to communion with other Christians even on this occasion.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This was effected by means of a few obscure bishops of that sect convening a Synod at the village of Pazum, which is situated near the sources of the river Sangarius; for there they framed a canon appointing its observance on the same day as that on which the Jews annually keep the feast of Unleavened Bread.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: An aged man, who was the son of a presbyter, and had been present with his father at this Synod, gave us our information on this matter.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But both Agelius, bishop of the Novatians at Constantinople, and Maximus of Nicæa, as also the bishops of Nicomedia and Cotyæum, were absent, although the ecclesiastical affairs of the Novatians were for the most part under the control of these bishops.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: How the church of the Novatians soon after was divided into two parties in consequence of this Synod, shall be related in its proper course: but we must now notice what took place about the same time in the Western parts.
